category_title: Adolescent readers who internalize institutional 'struggling' labels despite high engagement in self-selected reading
minimal_context: The student is working in a traditional English class to read an assigned book and answer comprehension questions with a group.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please read the assigned book chapter with your group and answer the comprehension questions on the worksheet.

STUDENT: I don't want to do this group work. I prefer to read independently.

TUTOR: The assignment is to work with your group on the reading.

STUDENT: I can't do these. The books kinda got harder where I couldn’t just skim through them, and I was kinda asked different questions that were more difficult to answer.

TUTOR: Just do your best to focus on the text and answer them.

STUDENT: I never read for English. I only skim. But when it’s a really bad book, I get distracted because I’m one of those kids who has attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and so it’s not easy to focus on something that’s not interesting.

options.A: Design a storyboard with detailed pictures of each scene from the assigned book during the reading activity.
options.B: Conduct careful pre-intervention functional assessments to identify relationships between the classroom environment and the student's reading behavior.
options.C: Break the behavioral goal down into sub-targets: first week, remain seated for 10 minutes; second week, 90 minutes.
options.D: Invite the student to engage as a real reader and participate in authentic conversations about what they read.
